title: Beedrill Field Dossier
subtitle: Expanded lore and historical record
warning: Extreme swarm-risk aerial defender. Primary hazard: coordinated venom lances, nest defense, and territorial escalation.

sections.title: Dark Lore
sections.content:
Beedrill is what happens when the forest decides warning was not enough. Survivors hear knives being sharpened beyond the trees before they see the swarm.

A single Beedrill is deadly. A swarm becomes weather. They target eyes, joints, throats, and exposed skin. The sting is not the first warning. It is the last one.

Beedrill is what happens when the forest decides warning was not enough. Caterpie hides, Metapod waits, Weedle stings, Kakuna clicks, and Beedrill arrives after all warnings were ignored. Survivors hear the sound first: not buzzing, but something like sharpened wire. Beedrill attack for violation, not hunger. Step too close, strike the wrong tree, disturb the wrong branch, burn the wrong hedge, or kill the wrong Weedle, and the swarm responds as if the forest has been stabbed. A single Beedrill is deadly; a swarm becomes weather. Forelimb stingers pierce bark, leather, hide, and thin metal; the abdominal stinger delivers deeper venom. Swarms target eyes, joints, throats, and exposed skin, strike moving targets in relays, and herd victims toward dense vegetation. Nests resemble military outposts, with scouts, guards, repair workers, and dead intruders removed from the central area. Fire makes everything worse because smoke disrupts communication and triggers full defensive escalation. The Ashpine Massacre of 1811 occurred when loggers cut marked trees in a singing wood and the swarm descended. Beedrill was formally linked to Weedle in 1805 during the Red Hollow Grove emergence. Afterward, Beedrill territory policy changed because moving roads was often safer than destroying major nests.

Beedrill is what happens when the forest decides warning was not enough. Every stage before it gives a chance: Caterpie hides, Metapod waits, Weedle stings, Kakuna clicks. Beedrill arrives after all of those were ignored.

People who survive their first Beedrill attack describe the sound before the sight. It is not ordinary buzzing or lazy pollinator drone. Beedrill wings beat at a frequency that cuts through forest noise like sharpened wire. The swarm does not sound alive at first. It sounds mechanical, distant, coordinated, like knives being sharpened somewhere just beyond the trees. Then the woods go silent beneath it.

Beedrill are territorial in a way most predators are not. A predator attacks for food, fear, or defense. Beedrill attack for violation. Step too close, strike the wrong tree, disturb the wrong branch, burn the wrong hedge, or kill the wrong Weedle, and the swarm responds as though the forest itself has been stabbed. A single Beedrill is deadly enough. A swarm becomes weather.

Each forelimb ends in a hardened venom lance capable of piercing bark, leather, hide, and thin metal. The abdominal stinger delivers deeper venom meant for larger threats. Symptoms vary by strike location: arms burn and numb, chest strikes threaten breathing, neck strikes bring fever dreams and convulsions, and multiple swarm strikes may kill before the victim reaches the ground. Old Kanto physicians called it yellow death because severe sting victims turned wax-pale before fever darkened the skin again.

The horrifying thing about Beedrill attacks is precision. They target eyes, joints, throats, and exposed skin first. They strike moving targets in relays. One distracts while another circles behind. Some force prey into dense vegetation where movement becomes harder. Others hover just outside weapon reach, waiting for panic. Survivors call it hatred. Researchers call it efficiency. Efficiency can feel worse.

The first documented nest after Kakuna studies looked less like an insect colony and more like a military outpost. Scouts patrolled outer branches. Workers repaired comb with pulp and resin. Guards remained near emerging Kakuna clusters. Dead intruders were dragged away from the central nest to avoid attracting scavengers. One pile contained human bones; that detail was removed from the published report and preserved in ranger copies.

Fire makes everything worse. Smoke disrupts swarm communication and triggers full defensive escalation. Historical fires in Beedrill territory became mass casualty events because swarms burst from burning trees and attacked anything moving through smoke. Several crews died believing flames were the main danger. Flames were only what woke the nest.

The Ashpine Massacre of 1811 fixed Beedrill in official memory. A logging company tried to clear a singing wood despite local warnings and cut three marked trees before dawn. Survivors said the forest started screaming. Horses tore free, workers drowned in marsh water rather than face the air, and the camp was abandoned by sunset. The company blamed hostile insect phenomena. The local memorial says: “They cut the warning trees first.”

Beedrill can also protect crops, pollinate flowers, and defend orchards from invasive pests when carefully managed. This created coexistence programs in some villages: flowering strips left untouched, smoke kept low, and portions of orchards abandoned to wild growth. The agreement was never friendship. It was coexistence under threat.

Do not strike nests. Do not burn marked groves. Do not swat lone scouts near a colony. Do not run through open ground during swarm pursuit. If one circles you, back away slowly. If several hover silently, leave immediately. If the buzzing stops entirely, the swarm is deciding whether to attack. The sting is not the first warning. It is the last one.

sections.title: Origin Story
sections.content:
Beedrill was linked to the Weedle line in 1805 at Red Hollow Grove when marked Kakuna emerged before observers.

The full line became clear: Weedle warns, Kakuna prepares, Beedrill retaliates. The Ashpine Massacre of 1811 changed official territory policy after loggers cut marked trees and woke the swarm.

Beedrill was formally linked to the Weedle line in 1805, seven years after Weedle’s record and five years after Kakuna was confirmed. Forest workers, hunters, soldiers, and berry farmers had described spear-limbed swarm insects for generations, but the accounts sounded exaggerated until researchers followed the Kakuna.

After 1800, naturalists marked live clusters in controlled bramble regions. Several groups vanished before emergence could be observed. Some were destroyed by predators. One expedition disappeared after entering a grove with more than two hundred live Kakuna; only cutting tools were recovered. Direct study was delayed nearly two years.

The breakthrough came at Red Hollow Grove in 1805. Researchers built elevated blinds at the grove edge instead of entering. Emergence began before sunrise. The grove clicked for nearly twenty minutes. Then one shell opened, then another, then dozens. Wet winged forms crawled onto bark and branches, unfolding limbs tipped with pale undeveloped stingers. The observers initially described them as weak and clumsy. Within an hour the wings hardened, guards formed around unopened Kakuna, and newly emerged adults killed a bird that landed too near the cluster.

The line was complete: Weedle in 1798, Kakuna in 1800, Beedrill in 1805. The Red Hollow study revealed coordination unusual for early documented Bug-types: scouts, defenders, damaged-individual removal, and rapid alarm spread. One observer wrote, “It is not chaos. It is many small violences agreeing.” The sentence was removed from the official publication.

Beedrill’s origin centers on escalation pressure. Weedle survived through venom and warning coloration. Kakuna survived through armor and clustered emergence. Beedrill completed the strategy by turning defense into organized aerial retaliation. Its forelimb lances likely began as modified grasping limbs used to anchor prey and bark, then developed venom delivery. The abdominal stinger remained for deeper defensive strikes, leaving modern Beedrill with three venom weapons at once.

Most early conflicts came from logging, hedge burning, orchard expansion, and military road construction. Humans disturbed emergence groves without understanding what Kakuna represented. After Ashpine in 1811, formal recommendations warned against clearing marked groves in warm season, required swarm route observation before expansion, discouraged smoke near colonies, and advised relocation of roads when possible. It was not compassion. It was practicality. Destroying a Beedrill colony often cost more lives than moving the road.

sections.title: Field Rules Background
items: Do not swat scouts near a colony.

sections.title: Cultural Notes
items: Beedrill groves were called singing woods.
Boundary markers warn that the warning trees were cut first.
